lesson.title: Interfaces
lesson.summary: Define behaviour with abstract types — let different types satisfy the same contract.

sections.heading: What is an interface
sections.bodyHtml:
An interface defines a set of method signatures. Any type whose methods match the interface satisfies it automatically — no explicit implements keyword.

type Speaker interface {
    Speak() string
}

type Dog struct{}
func (d Dog) Speak() string { return "Woof!" }

type Cat struct{}
func (c Cat) Speak() string { return "Meow" }

Both Dog and Cat satisfy Speaker. A function accepting Speaker can take either:

func greet(s Speaker) {
    fmt.Println(s.Speak())
}

greet(Dog{})  // Woof!
greet(Cat{})  // Meow

sections.heading: The empty interface
sections.bodyHtml:
The empty interface interface{} (write any in modern Go) has zero methods, so every type satisfies it. Use it sparingly — it bypasses type safety:

var x any = 42
x = "hello"
x = 3.14

To get a concrete value back, use a type assertion:

val := x.(int)       // panics if x is not int
val, ok := x.(int)   // ok=false instead of panic

sections.heading: Interface values
sections.bodyHtml:
An interface value holds two things: a concrete type and a value of that type. This is sometimes called the interface tuple:

var s Speaker = Dog{}
// (type: Dog, value: {})

You can check the underlying type with a type switch:

switch v := s.(type) {
case Dog:
    fmt.Println("dog")
case Cat:
    fmt.Println("cat")
default:
    fmt.Println("unknown")
}

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/interfaces/main.go.

Define a Shape interface with an Area() float64 method. Implement it on Circle (radius field) and Rectangle (width, height fields). Print the area of each.

questions.prompt: In Go, how does a type satisfy an interface?

options.label: By implementing all the interface's methods — no explicit keyword
An alias for interface{} — any type satisfies it

questions.prompt: What happens in a type assertion x.(int) if x does not hold an int?

options.label: Returns the zero value of int
Returns false as a second value

exercises.title: Shape interface
exercises.instructions: Implement Shape with Area() on Circle and Rectangle. Print the area of a circle with radius 5 and a rectangle 3x4.
